If the gap problem interests you, I recommend checking out Lamdu, which Cyrus forgot to mention as prior art.

I'm looking for an alternative to the overwhelming complexity of modern programming languages. How simple can a language be while still remaining viable, and how do we make languages easier to use? Some of my inspiration comes from research of the 1960's and '70s, before everyone was infected by the OOP virus. (The OOP fad led to a big increase in language complexity.) You mention Wirth: his Euler language is largely forgotten, but  is simpler and more powerful than Pascal, which focussed more on efficiency.

> bash for the cloud

you asked for feedback. I read that as horrible global variables and cryptic punctuation, lots of hacks needed to get things to actually work correctly. space sensitivity, lots of arcane knowledge needed to actually get things not the break on edge cases ... bash is one of the 3 worst programming environments I know. The only thing worse is DOS batch files. 

maybe that marketing speak works better for others.

Looks pretty interesting, though I'm not sure I quite follow. The 'getting started' page (<https://docs.storyscript.io/quick-start/#write-your-first-story>) just shows how to build a webserver, which doesn't feel like what Storyscript is for? You need a better example to show off its strengths. Maybe you receive a request and make a request elsewhere? Or make a request elsewhere and postprocess its results before sending them back?

"bash for the cloud" is fine by me. The slogan I'm bothered by is "top-level". Saying "top-level" implies that there can be no higher level, but then when I look at the code it looks a lot less high-level than IFTTT or Fantastical or most Racket DSLs. I suppose the meaning might be "this tool is the glue; it sits on top of everything it's gluing, and nothing is going to sit on top of it" but that's not the common interpretation of the spectrum of "low-level" and "high-level".

What bugs me about "top-level" is that history is full of people declaring that they've reached the endpoint of some spectrum. The term "atom" means "indivisible", because the atom is the most fundamental building block of all matter. Which is, of course, bullshit.

I wrote apps in Modula-2. What is special about Modula-2 modules vs Java or C# or any other modern language that doesn't use header files?

C++ has different goals IMO than many other languages which is one reason I value it. One of its goals is to generate optimal code. You can write very complex type safe classes with lots of methods and know that even though they've been specialized via templates and even though they're using all kinds of  other classes as helpers the code they generate when optimization is on is 1 or 2 instructions.

I don't know which other languages can make guarantees like that. AFAIK Java, C#, Swift, Modula-2 etc can not.

The only way to do that that I know of is either to use header files (or precompiled header files) since you need tons of knowledge at the time of code generation to do it, OR, put tons of code generation logic in the linker since it would be the only other place that kind of optimization would have access to enough knowledge to be able to do similar optimizations.

If you don't care about perf to that level suddenly you're open to a whole lot of other solutions. When I'm doing game dev or OS dev or driver dev I do care about that level of perf. When I'm doing app dev or web dev or server dev usually don't

Header files are widely recognized as creating a lot of problems. That's why C++20 will have modules. Read more about the problems with header files: <https://clang.llvm.org/docs/Modules.html#problems-with-the-current-model>

Modula2 was unique in that it used 2 different files for each module: one was the definition file, which is the exported to the outside world functions, types, constants, variables that are exported from a module, could be compiled, which emitted a .MSYM file, which could then be used by some other programmer who is going to use that module, without having access to the source code. And as long as the definition part didn't change, you could rebuild a huge program without recompiling the headers. It meant that someone could promise an interface, and as long as those promises didn't change the clients could be unconcerned with the internal workings. It doesn't pay off until you make a huge program, but as you know the N-squared aspect of programming complexity is present, and large Modula-2 programs were typically half of the the equivalent C program (and faster too, with many times lower nesting levels than Java programs). In Oberon he abandoned the practice of having a second separate file which did mean extra typing. But it did make compilation speedy, as you typically only change 1% of a program, and without any fancy incremental compilation features in the compiler (which can have errors) meant that compilation speeds were incredible compared to large C programs. I built some humongous products with it, like IBM DisplayWrite 5 Composer, Flying Colors, and Discus labeler, worked well on WIndows and Mac platforms. But again the module feature was just one aspect of Modula-2 that was great; it also had a much clearer definition syntax for a pointer to an array of functions that return a function that returns an integer. Try doing that in C, it is a mind-bender, while in Modula-2 it was clean and clear. The larger the program, the better Modula-2 looks, as its simplicity was born of clean logic. All of Wirth's work is elegant and very mathematical in its foundations.

All of Wirth's work is elegant and very mathematical in its foundations.

I don't see too many flaws at first glance. The big one that stands out is the usage of try/catch, which is a horrendous way to handle errors. You are going to have a ton of errors in your system, where the things you are connecting to are going to fail to respond in a timely manner. Intrinsically, these services are finite state machines, yet you have no syntax for FSM. This is a huge omission, and will come to haunt you later.  try/catch is not going to deliver the necessary granularity to debug flaky interactions.

 I would suggest elif for chaining IF statements as the extra typing is unnecessary.  I would suggest using NOT instead of !, and <> for not equal, as i do in beads as the exclamation point is one of the precious punctuation marks you might want to use later in your evolution.

As another minor note, it has proven dangerous from past experience to have a beginning block delimiter be identical the ending block delimiter. So ### should not be start and stop, because once you delete one of them, you have now flipped the meaning of what is commented versus not commented. So C uses /* ... */, Modula-2 uses (*... *), you might consider >>> .... <<< or some other symmetrical but not identical delimiter.

So C uses /* ... */, Modula-2 uses (*... *), you might consider >>> .... <<< or some other symmetrical but not identical delimiter.

And by the way when you click "debugging" in the docs you get a 404 page not found error. How you are going to debug these scripts is one of the key aspects of your system, since 80% of the time spent programming is consumed in the debug phase, and your language will be no different.

nice to see a time fundamental type, however, it appears that it doesn't cover calendar dates. I think this will cause immediate problems, as many business processes are tied to calendars, so you either need to extend time to include a date+ time, or add a date data type. The current unix cron syntax is horrendous, and i am sure you can improve upon this easily.

In the Beads language we offer 2 block comment syntaxes, and support nesting, because commenting out a big block of code that has comments inside is painful if nesting is not supported. C has this problem, and it is huge. We offer 2 different block comments because it is helpful when trying to find the end of a block if you only use a special one for big runs of code.

I could go on for hours about all the subtleties of supporting comments, and how it interacts with computer generated code, but one should always make it easy and convenient to put in comments, because lazy people don't do it. Comments are for the humans reading the code, the code is for the computer.

There is an abhorrent trend where relatively inexperienced programmers are now proudly stating they refuse to comment. If they had to work on some 300k line program left behind by sadists, they wouldn't be so proud of their lack of commenting. It a curse on future generations to do anything than your best effort to make it easier for the next person to understand your program.

I am not a fan of arrays based on 0. This is a hangover from C where an array was basically nothing more than an alias to a pointer, and a zero offset was the first element. There is not a human being who is taught to count starting with 0. If you tell a kid to count to five you go  1, 2, 3, 4, 5. So not sure why you are going to continue this unnatural practice of 0 based. Your language is interpreted. In Beads we start at 1 by default, however arrays are sparse and thus you can have negative subscripts, not to mention enumerated type subscripts. You probably don't need that kind of power, but since your language is targeting a very high level, it is incongruous to my thinking to inherit an assembly language level piece of thinking.

It is also inevitable that you will need to support breaking out of a nested loop, so you will need to have optional loop labels for that case where you want to continue the outer loop, or break out of nested loops.

It is also inevitable that you will need to support breaking out of a nested loop, so you will need to have optional loop labels for that case where you want to continue the outer loop, or break out of nested loops.

i also think the end command should probably have an optional error return code, as sub-scripts may fail in different ways, and being able to detect the kind of error will be occasionally needed. A script is like calling a function; it may need to pass information back up the chain.

As for C++ modules vs headers I get it. Header files and the preprocessor have their place though. Without them you'll end up having to write code generators to solve certain kinds of common problems. One example is using macro lists to generate enums, strings for those enums, dispatch tables to handlers for those enums, etc..

In most "modern" languages like C# or Java or Swift it's just assumed you're going to waste memory having code generate data at init time. In C++ it's entirely possible to get much of your data generated at compile time and just loaded at runtime. Those kinds of issues might not matter on my 16gig mac but they matter quite a bit on smaller devices.

Any future language that aims to amplify the industry to 10x the number of engineers and potentially the speed of development IMO must be focused on moving data: readable, intuitive, syntax-light, and transparent.

Any future language that aims to amplify the industry to 10x the number of engineers and potentially the speed of development IMO must be focused on moving data: readable, intuitive, syntax-light, and transparent.

Feedback can be tough because everyone thinks differently. Some engineers say “just show me the code” some say “more theory and marketing benefits”… Hard to find the one-size fits all. And the language is not a one-size-fits-all: it’s a language for moving data between services. Service-oriented programming at it’s core. Let high&low level languages do the heavy lifting. I hope this will make using Storyscript very appealing being a fully-polyglot development language.

Interesting project! The landing page should make it more clear, what business problems you help to solve, for whom and how. And make the use cases stick out better when skim-reading, as others pointed out.

The visual style of the site is good and the project is interesting, I’m sure that with small tweaks to copy text and placement, the understandability can be improved a lot.

I’d run a user test on the landing page too, via <http://Usertesting.com|Usertesting.com> or similar, to see if people understand what it is about. Or then do some lean user testing for it to the target audience in a coworking space, for example, or similar.

<https://www.usertesting.com>

RE “Move data” some love some meh LOL we like to say that businesses are simply stories data; how data moves from user to business to server to database and back (not always in that order hehe). But data from a “top-level” the business-logic level.

We are trying to position ourselves as the first business-logic language. Sure you can write business-logic in Python, but we are trying to show the world why Storyscript is better for BPM/logic and why Python (and all other languages) are better for data processing/algorithms/etc. — Storyscript is the most inclusive language because it’s polyglot development in a distributed monolith.
